passages.text: Deep Learning Empowered Microstructure Codebook: New Paradigm for Multi‐Parameter Tissue Characterization Estimation
ABSTRACT

Diffusion MRI (dMRI) enables the examination of microstructural profiles and tissue changes using specific microstructural modeling, but it requires long acquisition times and dense q‐space sampling. Current deep learning‐based methods are also limited by their inability to generalize across protocols and extend to new microstructural indices. This work introduces a novel framework that addresses these limitations by learning a microstructural codebook, facilitating accurate, rapid, and multi‐parameter microstructure imaging. Our approach integrates the spherical mean technique (SMT) with a hybrid Mamba‐CNN architecture and learnable tissue‐compartment kernels, effectively capturing multiscale spatial dependencies while linking spherical mean signals to biophysical microstructure models. This design enhances both interpretability and adaptability, enabling robust estimation of 24 microstructural metrics derived from 8 widely used biophysical diffusion models, even under undersampled acquisition conditions. Notably, the framework demonstrates strong generalization across diverse acquisition protocols and enables seamless adaptation to novel microstructural indices with minimal fine‐tuning, underscoring its flexibility and practical utility. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets confirm the method's superior accuracy, generalization, and transferability. This work presents a codebook‐driven framework for microstructure imaging that bridges biophysical modeling and deep learning to enable more interpretable and adaptable dMRI analysis. The code is available at https://github.com/1nlandempire/Microstructure‐codebook‐imaging.
Key Points
A learnable microstructure codebook maps parameterized spherical‐mean signals to multiple biophysical indices, enabling high‐quality multi‐parameter estimation from undersampled dMRI.
Built on a hybrid Mamba‐CNN network, we introduce a novel parameterization of the spherical‐mean signal that substantially improves generalization across b‐values and acquisition protocols.
The pretrained codebook supports minimal fine‐tuning to new microstructural indices, improving extensibility and interpretability.

We propose DEMIC, a deep‐learning microstructure codebook framework for dMRI microstructure imaging: (1) accurate multi‐parameter estimation from undersampled data; (2) robust cross‐protocol and cross‐model generalization; and (3) flexible transfer to new microstructural indices via fine‐tuning.
Introduction

Diffusion MRI (dMRI)‐based biophysical diffusion models play a vital role in characterizing complex changes in tissue microstructure, such as dendrites, axons, and glial cells, providing essential insights into the structural basis of the human brain (Mori and Zhang ). Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) (Basser et al. ), which simplifies fitting a Gaussian diffusion model from single‐shell dMRI (single b‐value), is already widely used to identify pathological changes in the brain. However, DTI measures diffusion anisotropy by fitting a Gaussian model to single‐shell dMRI, which conflates the effects of microscopic‐level neurite anisotropy and orientation dispersion. Advancements in MRI hardware, increased computational power, and progress in neuroscience have driven the development of more sophisticated dMRI biophysical models, such as neurite orientation dispersion and density imaging (NODDI) (Zhang et al. ), multi‐compartment microscopic diffusion imaging (MCMDI) (Kaden, Kelm, et al. ), free water diffusion tensor imaging (FwDTI) (Henriques et al. ), and soma and neurite density imaging (SANDI) (Palombo et al. ), which depend on the advanced acquisition protocol to provide more specific biomarkers. Advanced biophysical models relate the diffusion‐weighted signal protocol to the underlying tissue microstructure, thereby capturing more realistic tissue properties, such as cell size, membrane permeability, and the geometry of cellular microenvironments. Enhancing model performance is a persistent challenge due to complex acquisition protocols, long scanning times, intensive data processing, and limited specificity in distinguishing neurological conditions, all of which significantly constrain the clinical applicability of imaging technologies.

Many studies have been proposed to probe the tissue microarchitecture of the human brain. Biophysical model‐based fitting approaches (Daducci et al. ; Fick et al. ; Garyfallidis et al. ; Liu et al. ) rely on predefined biophysical assumptions (Zhang et al. ; Palombo et al. ; Fieremans et al. ; Le Bihan et al. ; Neto Henriques ) and employ nonlinear least‐squares or Bayesian methods to fit dMRI signals, thereby providing microstructural biomarkers. Additionally, advanced biophysical modeling techniques, such as diffusion basis spectrum imaging (DBSI) (Wang et al. ), restricted spectrum imaging (RSI) (White et al. ), and spherical mean spectrum imaging (SMSI) (Huynh et al. ), provide sophisticated means to probe the tissue microarchitecture. DBSI probes tissue microstructure by decomposing the dMRI signal into a combination of isotropic and anisotropic components. RSI extends the linear spherical deconvolution model to probe tissue‐orientation structures across multiple length scales. SMSI overcomes some of the limitations of RSI and DBSI, utilizes the spherical mean technique (SMT) (Kaden, Kruggel, and Alexander ) to factor out fiber‐orientation effects, and provides more comprehensive microstructural insights. Despite their strengths, these methods typically require extensive computational resources and densely sampled multi‐shell q‐space data.

Various deep learning methods have been proposed to address these challenges by reducing reliance on densely sampled q‐space data and accelerating data processing (Zheng et al. ; HashemizadehKolowri et al. ; Golkov et al. ; Karimi and Gholipour ; Gibbons et al. ; Tian et al. ; Chen et al. ; Ye et al. ; Wang et al. ). These methods propose learning a nonlinear mapping from undersampled dMRI data with a few diffusion gradient directions to microstructural maps. The q‐space deep learning was first proposed by Golkov et al.  to directly map the voxel‐wise dMRI signals to microstructure parameters using fewer b‐values and gradient directions. A three‐layer multi‐layer perceptron (MLP) was used to estimate diffusion kurtosis imaging (DKI) (Jensen et al. ) measures from only 12 diffusion directions and NODDI measures from only 8 diffusion directions. Using a deep 3D convolutional neural network (CNN), DeepDTI (Tian et al. ) addresses a more complex problem by estimating high‐quality DTI parameters from only one b = 0 and 6 diffusion‐weighted images (DWIs). Chen et al.  presented a compressive sense‐based deep learning method to estimate high‐angular (HA) diffusion data from low‐angular data acquired with fewer gradients. Using reconstructed HA dMRI data yields more accurate estimates of NODDI‐derived indices. A transformer‐based network (Karimi and Gholipour ) was presented to accurately estimate the diffusion tensor from six diffusion‐weighted images by exploiting the correlations between the dMRI signal and DTI metrics in neighboring voxels.

Estimation of reliable tissue microstructure from low‐quality dMRI signals is another challenge. Super‐resolved q‐DL (SR‐q‐DL) (Qin, Liu, et al. ) was designed to accurately estimate high‐resolution tissue microstructure from low‐quality dMRI data acquired at low spatial resolution. SR‐q‐DL exploits the sparsity of diffusion signals and integrates it with convolutional operations to improve estimation accuracy. Karimi et al.  developed a method for probing tissue microstructure from low‐quality fetal dMRI data. Using high‐quality newborn and low‐quality fetal data, the proposed method can generate synthetic fetal data paired with high‐quality microstructural indexes for training. The network trained on the generated data can accurately estimate tissue microstructure from low‐quality fetal data. MSR‐q‐DL (Qin, Li, et al. ) has been proposed to improve the estimation of high‐resolution (HR) tissue microstructure using low‐resolution (LR) dMRI data by introducing HR information from an additional modality. In high‐b‐value DWI acquisitions, the derived volumes typically have a signal‐to‐noise ratio (SNR). Noise in DWIs reduces the accuracy of DTI‐derived microstructural parameters, thereby prolonging acquisition time to achieve higher SNR. Tian et al.  proposed a self‐supervised deep learning approach, SDnDTI, for estimating high‐SNR DTI parameters from low‐SNR dMRI data. SDnDTI does not require additional high‐SNR data for training supervision, thereby supporting a broader range of clinical practice and neuroscience studies.

Modeling of advanced microstructural indices relies on multi‐shell dMRI signals. Several methods have recently been developed to probe brain tissue microarchitecture from single‐shell (single b‐value) dMRI signals. By defining biophysical models to interpret dMRI signals, these studies can extract microstructural parameters from single b‐value dMRI. Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) (Basser et al. ), which simplifies fitting a Gaussian diffusion model to single‐shell dMRI data, is already widely used to identify pathological changes in the brain. Several deep learning methods have been proposed to accurately estimate DTI parameters from undersampled q‐space data (Karimi and Gholipour ; Tian et al. ; Li et al. ). However, most deep learning (DL) methods generalize poorly on data outside the training distribution. Building on Recurrent Inference Machines, Sabidussi et al.  proposed a deep learning method to produce reliable diffusion tensor estimates across datasets acquired under diverse settings. Advanced dMRI models interpret dMRI signals with multiple compartments to better detect subtle changes in brain tissues. Due to the complex structure of the biophysical model, advanced dMRI models beyond DTI usually require densely sampled q‐space data (i.e., dMRI data of multiple b‐values and diffusion directions in the q‐space). Nath et al.  proposed a method to capture standard intrinsic features across multiple dMRI models, from single‐shell DW‐MRI, to characterize multiple compartmental microstructural measures. Metrics typically estimated from multi‐shell q‐space data, such as NODDI‐ and intravoxel incoherent motion (IVIM)‐derived metrics, can be estimated from single‐shell dMRI data alone. A dictionary‐based method was proposed by Faiyaz et al.  for deriving NODDI metrics from single‐shell dMRI data. This method uses dictionary learning to estimate f  iso  as a prior. Subsequently, the neurite density index (NDI) and orientation dispersion index (ODI) can be accurately estimated from single‐shell q‐space data using the known f  iso  as prior information (Faiyaz et al. ).

dMRI microstructural imaging can help us understand brain development, maturation, and pathological alterations in neurological disorders (Santini et al. ; Su et al. ; Peng et al. ). More recently, some studies have proposed methods for microstructure estimation in specific groups, such as patients with tumors and stroke. For microstructural estimation in stroke patients, Gibbons et al.  employed a 2D CNN‐based method to produce accurate generalized fractional anisotropy and NODDI parameter maps from undersampled q‐space data. The estimated parameter maps can be used to predict stroke functional outcome measures for clinical evaluations. Based on this work, Gibbons et al.  proposed three networks to jointly estimate 11 microstructural metrics from a subset of the dMRI data, and the performance of the three networks was evaluated (HashemizadehKolowri et al. ). After training, the networks were evaluated on stroke patients, demonstrating that the DL method can estimate diffusion maps in the stroke region with quality comparable to that in other regions. Focusing on tissue microstructure estimation in babies, Chen et al.  presented a graph convolutional neural network to estimate NODDI parameters from undersampled data, with additional consideration of q‐space angular neighboring information to improve estimation accuracy. To better assess the spatially heterogeneous changes in the human brain during the initial years of life, Huynh et al.  proposed a method for quantifying tissue microstructure by characterizing water diffusion across a spectrum of length scales, accounting for orientation dispersion. The proposed method from the Baby Connectome Project can be applied to assess the microarchitecture of infant brains. Karimi et al.  proposed a method to generate large amounts of reliable fetal data for training by combining fetal and high‐quality premature infant scans. The synthesized data were used to train a CNN to estimate color fractional anisotropy with high accuracy for probing fetal brain microarchitecture.

Although there have been many studies proposing different methods for microstructure imaging, the estimation of microstructural maps still faces several interconnected challenges: (1) to obtain high‐quality results, advanced dMRI techniques require densely sampled data in q‐space along different b‐values, which can significantly increase the data acquisition time and signal distortion; (2) estimation of microscopic features is confounded by the effects of fiber crossing and dispersion; (3) dMRI models are highly non‐linear and computationally expensive. Each diffusion technique yields only a limited set of microstructural indices, making it impractical to use multiple models to derive additional properties for clinical applications; (4) deep learning‐based methods of microstructure estimation often lack interpretability due to the direct mapping of dMRI signals to microstructural parameters and the “black‐box” nature of the neural networks. Interpretability is crucial for clinical decision support, validation, regulatory compliance, and improving model performance. The interpretability of microstructure estimation methods facilitates their practical clinical application; (5) DL‐based methods lack extensibility, as they are restricted to the microstructural indices included during training; (6) some dMRI models, like SANDI, depend on ultra‐high b‐value acquisitions, making it challenging to estimate SANDI‐derived indices from low b‐value data. These interrelated limitations highlight the need for a more flexible and generalizable imaging approach.

This work introduces a novel approach for accurate, rapid, and multi‐parameter imaging of microstructures across various acquisition protocols with a Deep learning Empowered Microstructure Codebook (DEMIC). Figure 1 provides an overview of the workflow. DEMIC features the innovative concept of a microstructural codebook, a learned set of overcomplete tissue‐compartment kernels that serve as a basis (or dictionary) linking parameterized spherical‐mean signals to multiple microstructural indices. These kernels encode a spectrum of diffusion scales, and target indices are obtained by a weighted combination of these kernels. To improve robustness to undersampling, we use the spherical mean technique (SMT) to remove orientation dependencies and introduce a novel voxel‐wise parameterization of the spherical mean signal using a three‐parameter exponential model, providing a compact, protocol‐agnostic representation. This parameterized representation serves as a compact, protocol‐agnostic input to a hybrid deep neural network that combines convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for local feature extraction with Mamba‐based spatial‐sequential modeling, enabling more efficient modeling of long‐range dependencies than conventional transformer‐ or LSTM‐based designs. Using the pretrained codebook, the method allows seamless adaptation to new microstructural metrics with minimal fine‐tuning. DEMIC boasts several key characteristics: (1) it enables the derivation of up to 24 microstructure metrics from 8 widely used biophysical diffusion models in clinical studies, utilizing undersampled dMRI data while maintaining high accuracy; (2) it is not reliant on a specific b‐value acquisition protocol, significantly enhancing its generalization capabilities across different datasets; (3) it provides a structured and interpretable framework for characterizing various dMRI biophysical models; and (4) it allows for the estimation of metrics derived from high b‐value dMRI using only low b‐value data, increasing its practicality for diverse clinical and research applications. These components collectively establish DEMIC as a unified and extensible paradigm for microstructure imaging, integrating SMT, SMS parameterization, the codebook, and Mamba modeling into a cohesive, practical framework. This framework supports existing metrics and enables efficient fine‐tuning of new ones, thereby greatly enhancing extensibility (Figure 2). We conducted experiments on datasets from various acquisition protocols and health conditions to demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method for joint microstructure estimation across different data acquisition scenarios.

Overall workflow. The spherical mean of original DWIs is continuously parameterized as features  and  to enhance dMRI signal representation. Based on the continuous  and  features, a hybrid architecture is designed by integrating Mamba‐based sequential modeling with CNN‐based local feature extraction to capture global and local spatial dependencies. A learnable microstructure codebook is constructed to represent multiple dMRI models in a shared parameter space, enabling multi‐parameter microstructure estimation.

Microstructure transfer learning. A pre‐trained microstructural codebook is obtained from large‐scale data to encode generalizable representations. The final 1 × 1 convolutional output heads are replaced to predict new microstructural indices. Fine‐tuning is performed with a limited dataset, enabling efficient adaptation to novel microstructure metrics with minimal supervision.
Results
Performance Comparison With Existing Methods

In this experiment, we employed 60 DWIs per subject, drawn from the SUDMEX dataset, which includes 4 gradient directions: b = 0 s/mm2, 28 b = 1000 s/mm2, and 28 b = 3000 s/mm2. The study involved 60 healthy controls (HCs), with 40 assigned to training and validation and 20 to independent testing. Rather than manually optimizing the gradient scheme, we used the initial gradient directions for each b‐value, as retrieved from the dataset. The quantitative results in Table 1 indicate that our method surpasses all baseline models, including MLP (Golkov et al. ), CNN (HashemizadehKolowri et al. ), UNET (Falk et al. ), UNET++ (Zhou et al. ), MESC‐SD (Ye et al. ), and JEMAP (Wang et al. ). Our approach achieved the highest peak signal‐to‐noise ratio (PSNR) and structural similarity index measure (SSIM) across nearly all microstructural metrics. Notably, we recorded PSNR values of 31.35 and 26.24 for the NODDI‐derived metrics free water fraction (FWF) and neurite density index (NDI), respectively, outperforming the closest competitor, UNET++, which attained PSNR values of 29.99 and 25.34 for the same indices.
Quantitative evaluation on SUDMEX with 60 DWIs using PSNR and SSIM.

Furthermore, our method consistently excels in challenging metrics such as mean signal diffusion kurtosis imaging (MSDKI)‐derived axonal volume fraction (F), intrinsic diffusivity (DI), IVIM‐derived perfusion fraction (f), and MCMDI‐derived intrinsic diffusivity (λ), demonstrating its superior performance in accurately estimating microstructure under undersampled acquisition conditions. Figure 3 summarizes quantitative comparisons across methods. In this figure, the vertical axis lists the different models, while the horizontal axis lists the evaluated microstructural indices. For each index, the error map to the right visualizes the voxel‐wise absolute difference between the estimated result and the high‐quality reference. The visual comparison of the estimated microstructure maps with reference images shows that our method effectively preserves sharp anatomical details. The error maps are less noisy and exhibit a more uniform and minimal distribution, indicating improved fidelity in capturing subtle variations in tissue microarchitecture. These advantages are particularly pronounced in metrics such as mean kurtosis (MK+), intra‐neurite volume fraction (Vint), and FWF, for which our method consistently achieves lower estimation errors. Both quantitative and qualitative assessments affirm the effectiveness of our approach. Despite relying on only three parameterized spherical mean signal (SMS) maps, our model produces results that rival and often exceed those obtained from methods utilizing the native full q‐space dMRI, highlighting the efficacy of our parameterized SMS representation in capturing sufficient information for precise microstructure estimation.
Qualitative evaluation of the performance comparison on the SUDMEX dataset with 60 DWIs.

Beyond the performance comparison shown above, we also conducted statistical confidence analyses using bootstrap resampling to assess the robustness of all competing methods (see Section S2 and Tables S2–S6). Additionally, an ablation experiment isolating the contribution of the Mamba block (see Section S3 and Table S7) confirms that incorporating the Mamba Block consistently improves microstructure estimation accuracy.
Evaluation of Model Robustness on Highly Undersampled Data

We conducted an undersampling experiment utilizing the SUDMEX dataset to thoroughly assess the robustness of our proposed method under constrained acquisition conditions. Five distinct DWI undersampling schemes were systematically tested: 60, 50, 40, 30, and 15 DWIs, corresponding to specific numbers of gradient directions on the shells at b = 0, 1000, and 3000 s/mm2. The configurations for each scheme were as follows: (1) 60 DWIs: 4, 28, 28 directions; (2) 50 DWIs: 4, 23, 23 directions; (3) 40 DWIs: 4, 18, 18 directions; (4) 30 DWIs: 2, 14, 14 directions; (5) 15 DWIs: 1, 7, 7 directions. Four random sampling strategies (designated as SS) of gradient directions (SS2–SS5) were employed for each undersampling scheme during testing. At the same time, SS1 used gradient directions consistent with those used during training. All other training settings were maintained using our previous experimental framework. This section focuses on performance comparisons using SS1, in which the training and inference gradient directions are aligned. Figure 4 summarizes the PSNR performance across 60, 50, 40, 30, and 15 gradient directions. The horizontal axis denotes the number of DWIs used during training and testing, while the vertical axis lists different sampling strategies (SS1–SS5). Figure 4 shows that our method outperforms the baseline across most microstructural indices, even with fewer gradient directions. Remarkably, our method delivers accurate estimates even under minimal undersampling (15 DWIs). For example, it achieves a PSNR of 30.26 for MK+ derived from DKI+, thereby exceeding the MLP's 29.17 and the CNN's 29.68. This highlights the robustness of our method in preserving microstructural fidelity under constrained acquisition scenarios.
Quantitative evaluation on the SUDMEX dataset with 15–60 gradient directions and five sampling schemes (SS1–SS5) using PSNR.

We focused on two widely used NODDI‐derived indices to evaluate robustness in clinically realistic settings: FWF and NDI. These metrics are widely employed in clinical and translational neuroscience studies due to their established biological relevance and interpretability (Kraguljac et al. ; Kamagata et al. ; Fu et al. ). Moreover, in real‐world clinical protocols, acquisition time is typically constrained, limiting the number of DWIs that can be collected. Therefore, we selected FWF and NDI to assess the robustness of our model under limited acquisition conditions, simulating scenarios commonly encountered in clinical settings. As illustrated in Figure 5, the box plots show SSIM values for FWF and NDI across seven methods, involving 20 test subjects, under the aforementioned five DWI sampling schemes. Our method consistently achieved higher SSIM scores and exhibited lower variability across all sampling conditions, indicating improved estimation stability and accuracy, even under minimal acquisition settings. These findings underscore the robustness of our method, making it well‐suited to practical applications in which the number of diffusion directions is inherently limited by scanning time or acquisition constraints.
Quantitative comparison of SSIM for FWF and NDI on the SUDMEX dataset under five DWI undersampling schemes across 20 subjects.
Robustness to Changes in Gradient Directions

We thoroughly evaluated our model's robustness by investigating its performance in scenarios in which the gradient directions during testing differed from those used during training. In this experiment, the number of DWIs was held constant across the training and testing phases (e.g., 60); however, the specific sets of gradient directions were modified. We compared five distinct sampling strategies (SS1–SS5), as depicted in Figure 4. SS1 corresponds to the condition in which the training and testing gradient directions are aligned, while SS2–SS5 use alternative sets of gradient directions for testing.

The findings in Figure 4 demonstrate that contemporary deep learning methods exhibit a marked performance degradation when confronted with mismatched gradient directions (SS2–SS5), as evidenced by lower PSNR values across various microstructural indices. For instance, the CNN method's performance on FWF decreases from 27.95 (SS1) to 25.78 (SS3). At the same time, the PSNR for the UNET model about NDI diminishes from 25.21 to 24.40 under analogous mismatch conditions. This observation accentuates their sensitivity to directional variations. Conversely, our methodological framework demonstrates either stable or improved performance under these mismatches; specifically, the PSNR for FWF increases from 31.35 (SS1) to 31.71 (SS3), while NDI increases from 26.24 to 26.27, and Mean Diffusivity (MD+) increases from 38.23 to 38.65. Such results underscore the inherent resilience of our parameterized SMS representation to angular sampling variations, thereby fostering robust generalization without overfitting to a specific set of diffusion directions.

Figure 6 presents qualitative results on the SUDMEX dataset for the specified 60 DWI setting, comparing the estimated microstructural maps obtained from testing with matched gradient directions (SS1) with those obtained with mismatched gradient directions (SS2). For each metric, the accompanying error map shows the absolute difference relative to the high‐quality reference. The comparison reveals that the alternative methods (e.g., MLP, CNN, UNET) exhibit augmented error rates when tested on unseen gradient directions (SS2), as indicated by the brighter error maps in the second column of each method pair (e.g., MLP+, CNN+, UNET+). In contrast, our approach and the SMT‐based JEMAP maintain minimal performance degradation and consistently produce dark error maps across variations in gradient direction, thereby facilitating stable and reliable microstructural estimation even under inconsistent acquisition conditions.
When the input gradient directions changed (SS2), a representative qualitative evaluation of the SUDMEX dataset with 60 DWIs was performed.

Moreover, we assessed the flexibility and robustness of our approach across scenarios in which the number of DWIs varied between the training and testing phases. Traditional deep learning methods, including MLP, CNN, and MESC‐SD, require an equal number of DWIs for training and inference, limiting their applicability in variable‐acquisition settings. In contrast, our model, characterized by parameterized SMS representations, provides consistent, uniform input to the network, regardless of the number of DWIs initially used. For example, a model trained on 60 DWIs can effectively evaluate various quantities of DWIs during testing (e.g., 60, 50, 40, 30, or 15), offering substantial practical advantages. Figure 7 illustrates model generalization under acquisition mismatch conditions. The horizontal axis indicates the number of DWIs used for training, and the vertical axis indicates the number of DWIs used for testing. As illustrated in Figure 7, only JEMAP and our method provide comprehensive evaluation results across all combinations of training and testing DWI counts, confirming their ability to generalize under acquisition‐mismatch conditions. Our methodology consistently yields elevated PSNR values across diverse testing configurations, with only marginal performance degradation even under severe undersampling. For instance, when tested on 30 DWIs using a model trained on 60 DWIs, our method achieves a PSNR of 31.53 on MK+, slightly lower than the 33.41 achieved under matching conditions. These results further substantiate the robustness and adaptability of our approach across varied acquisition scenarios, thereby enhancing its applicability in clinical or research environments where acquisition protocols differ significantly.
Quantitative evaluation of the SUDMEX dataset with varying gradient direction count during training (x‐axis) and testing (y‐axis) using PSNR.
Cross‐Dataset Generalization to Varying Acquisition Protocols and Pathologies

To assess the generalization capability of our method, we undertook cross‐dataset testing utilizing the BTC dataset, employing a model trained on the SUDMEX dataset, which comprised 40 healthy controls and 60 DWIs. The BTC testing cohort comprised 20 healthy control subjects, with 60 DWIs acquired at b‐values of 0, 700, 1200, and 2800 s/mm2, corresponding to 4, 16, 18, and 22 gradient directions, respectively. This acquisition protocol differs significantly from SUDMEX's, enabling a robust evaluation of our method's performance in novel acquisition settings.

As shown in Table 2, our method consistently achieved the highest PSNR and the lowest normalized root‐mean‐square error (NRMSE) across all microstructural metrics assessed. For instance, for the NODDI‐derived FWF and NDI indices, our method achieved PSNR values of 23.58 and 26.39 dB, respectively, exceeding the second‐best method by substantial margins. Moreover, for additional indices such as MK+, DI, and mean squared displacement (MSD), our method achieved PSNR values of 30.85, 33.99, and 28.66, respectively, along with remarkably low NRMSE values of 0.09 for MD+, 0.072 for λ, and 0.016 for axonal water fraction (AWF). These results substantiate the strong quantitative generalization performance of our approach.
Quantitative evaluation on 20 HCs from the BTC dataset with 60 DWIs using PSNR and NRMSE.

The findings further validate that the microstructural codebook derived from the SUDMEX dataset can be effectively extrapolated to the BTC dataset, underscoring the cross‐protocol robustness of our methodology. The qualitative results in Figure 8 support these conclusions, demonstrating that our method produces sharper, more anatomically accurate microstructural maps, thereby preserving intricate tissue structures without requiring retraining on the new acquisition protocol. This enhanced qualitative consistency underscores the practical applicability of our method across diverse clinical contexts.
Qualitative evaluation of the generalizability of HC from the BTC dataset with 60 DWIs.

Additionally, we evaluated our pretrained model on brain tumor patients from the BTC dataset to further validate our method's generalization in complex pathological scenarios. As illustrated in Figure 9, we assessed estimation accuracy by calculating Pearson correlation coefficients between reference microstructural indices and our predictions within tumor regions. Despite being exclusively trained on healthy controls, our method exhibited strong correlations (e.g., r = 0.98 for FWF, r = 0.97 for axonal volume fraction (F), and r = 0.93 for hindered axial diffusivity (Hindered AD)), suggesting that the learned codebook generalizes effectively to complex pathological tissues. These results highlight the potential of our approach for clinical applications involving significant structural abnormalities.
Evaluation of generalization on brain tumor patients from the BTC dataset with 60 DWIs.

We further evaluated the robustness of our method under varying echo times using a multi‐TE dMRI dataset (see Section S1, Table S1, and Figure S1). The results demonstrate that our model generalizes well across TE variations despite being trained on a single‐TE setting.
Transferring to New Microstructure Indices With Fine‐Tuning

We evaluated the transferability of our learned microstructural codebook to novel microstructural indices using a pre‐training‐fine‐tuning approach. Initially, our model was pre‐trained on a substantial dataset comprising 200 subjects from HCP, focusing on predicting 13 microstructural indices derived from five distinct dMRI models: MSDKI, FwDTI, IVIM, white matter tract integrity (WMTI), and 3D Simple Harmonic Oscillator‐based Reconstruction and Estimation (3D‐SHORE). To ensure the robustness of our findings, 10% of the dataset was reserved for validation purposes.

In the fine‐tuning phase, we adapted the pre‐trained model to predict additional microstructure indices, namely NODDI, DKI+, and MCMDI. We modified the final output layer to correspond to the target index count. This fine‐tuning involved data from only 2 SUDMEX subjects, comprising 60 DWIs acquired at b‐values of 0, 1000, and 3000 s/mm2. Testing was conducted on an independent cohort of 10 healthy control subjects from the SUDMEX dataset. In comparison, baseline methods were trained on a dataset of 5 SUDMEX subjects comprising 60 DWIs.

As shown in Table 3, our method consistently outperformed baseline techniques, achieving higher PSNR and lower learned perceptual image patch similarity (LPIPS) across all evaluated metrics. These results substantiate the efficacy of our learned codebook in capturing generalizable microstructural features, thereby facilitating efficient adaptation to novel dMRI models with minimal supervision. This underscores the promise of our model as a robust foundational tool for microstructural imaging across diverse dMRI models and various clinical settings.

Additionally, we validated the transferability of the pre‐trained codebook to the SANDI model on the HCP‐MGH dataset, which yields detailed microstructural indices from high‐b‐value dMRI acquisitions. High‐quality SANDI metrics were obtained from full‐sampled DWIs, with b‐values of 5000 and 10,000 s/mm2 using the AMICO, which served as the reference (ground truth) for evaluating our low‐b‐value reconstructions. The SANDI model estimates multiple microstructural parameters, including Fextra (extra‐cellular volume fraction), Fneurite (intra‐neurite volume fraction), Fsoma (intra‐soma volume fraction), Rsoma (apparent soma radius), De (extra‐cellular mean diffusivity), and Din (neurite axial diffusivity). Together, these metrics enable a detailed characterization of tissue architecture, which is critical for understanding brain cytoarchitecture and its variations.

Initialized with pre‐trained weights from the HCP dataset, our model was fine‐tuned solely on low‐b‐value DWIs (b = 0, 1000, and 3000 s/mm2) from 20 subjects in the HCP‐MGH dataset. Specifically, 168 DWI volumes were used, comprising 40, 64, and 64 DWI volumes at b = 0, 1000, and 3000 s/mm2, respectively. The output layer was subsequently modified to predict six SANDI‐derived metrics. We directly fitted the SANDI model to the same low‐b‐value data for baseline comparison. Testing on an additional cohort of 10 subjects revealed that our approach markedly enhanced the estimation of SANDI metrics under low‐b‐value acquisition conditions. As demonstrated in Figure 10, our method produced microstructural maps that closely aligned with the high‐quality reference data, achieving substantial PSNR gains across all six metrics and providing clearer, more structurally consistent results than baseline methods. These findings underscore that our α, β, and γ representations can encode SMS decay patterns from low‐b‐value DWIs, facilitating the implicit extrapolation of ultra‐high‐b‐value characteristics and accurate SANDI analysis.
Evaluation of the transferability on the HCP‐MGH dataset using PSNR.
Analysis of Learned Microstructure Codebook

Various studies have demonstrated the feasibility of investigating tissue microarchitecture by characterizing water diffusion across multiple length scales (White et al. ; Huynh et al. ; Wu ). Instead of using a user‐defined spin model, our method learns a microstructural codebook of 128 overcomplete tissue‐compartment kernels that map the parameterized spherical mean signal to widely used microstructure maps, enabling explainable estimation of multiple biological models. As shown in Figure 11, the learned kernels exhibit distinct spatial patterns with low inter‐kernel redundancy, enabling rich and compact representations of tissue heterogeneity. These kernels can be interpreted as a basis set capturing the proportions of spin packets undergoing fine‐to‐coarse‐scale diffusion processes. The structural diversity and decorrelation among kernels suggest that the model learned a biologically meaningful, information‐efficient representation of the diffusion process, consistent with biophysical modeling principles.
The part of the microstructure codebook learned by our method.

We performed a quantitative analysis to further investigate the robustness and pattern of the learned microstructure codebook by varying the number of kernels through modifications to the network architecture. Figure 12 depicts the pairwise Pearson correlation coefficients (absolute values) among the learned kernels across configurations with 256, 128, 64, and 32 kernels. Heat maps visualize the inter‐kernel relationships for each setting, and the mean absolute Pearson correlation coefficient () is computed as a quantitative metric of redundancy within the codebook. The analysis reveals that  varies with kernel count. Notably, the 128‐kernel configuration yields the lowest  = 0.286, suggesting a higher degree of decorrelation among kernels than other settings. Besides, Figure 12 reveals that complete linear independence among kernels remains elusive across all configurations. Whether employing the default 128 kernels, the expanded 256 kernels, or the reduced 64‐ and 32‐kernel sets, a small subset of elements consistently exhibits higher absolute Pearson correlation coefficients. This persistent partial correlation indicates that spin packets remain interdependent across diffusion scales, reflecting the complex interplay between fine‐ and coarse‐scale diffusion within the tissue microstructure.

Discussion

This study presents DEMIC, a novel dMRI‐based microstructure imaging framework that integrates a learnable codebook with the spherical mean technique and a hybrid Mamba‐CNN architecture. By leveraging a learnable codebook, our approach aims to enhance the interpretability, generalization, and transferability of microstructural estimates. We employed the SMT alongside a new parameterized representation method to achieve accurate, efficient, and robust microstructure estimations across various acquisition protocols. Furthermore, we developed a hybrid Mamba‐CNN architecture that captures multi‐scale spatial dependencies and effectively maps spherical mean signals to biophysical models. Experimental results indicate that our framework outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy and robustness, and that it enables seamless adaptation to new microstructural indices with minimal fine‐tuning.

The learnable codebook is the cornerstone of our framework, enabling the model to characterize multiple dMRI biophysical models by learning an overcomplete set of tissue‐compartment kernels (White et al. ; Huynh et al. ; Wu ). In contrast to previous approaches that rely on user‐defined spin models, our method adaptively learns an expressive microstructure codebook that maps SMS to various microstructural indices (as shown in Table 1), yielding a more interpretable assessment of microstructural properties. The learned kernels reveal distinct spatial patterns with minimal inter‐kernel redundancy, effectively capturing the proportions of spin packets undergoing diffusion across scales from fine to coarse (see Figure 11). This observation aligns with earlier studies that suggest characterizing water diffusion across multiple length scales enhances the interpretability of the method. Moreover, our approach promotes transferability by enabling effective adaptation to predict novel microstructural indices with limited fine‐tuning and minimal data (as shown in Table 3 and Figure 10). Thus, this framework represents a promising foundation for advancing microstructure imaging.

We have employed SMT to account for orientation dispersion, thereby enhancing the robustness of microstructure estimation across diverse acquisition protocols. A pertinent concern is the potential negative impact of SMT's inherent dimensionality reduction on the accuracy of microstructural estimates. Existing literature has demonstrated that the spherical mean technique facilitates microstructure estimation without compromising the fidelity of fiber crossing and dispersion. This may improve sensitivity and specificity in assessing various neurological conditions (Kaden, Kelm, et al. ; Neto Henriques ; Huynh et al. ; Kaden, Kruggel, and Alexander ; Wang et al. ; Wu ; Tristán‐Vega et al. ). In our experimental evaluations of estimation accuracy, the SMT‐based approach has demonstrated superior performance compared to methodologies that use raw DWI signals, such as q‐DL (Golkov et al. ). This superiority is evidenced by lower absolute estimation errors and elevated PSNR for NODDI parameters (see Figure 3 and Table 1). These findings suggest that the SMT retains sufficient information for accurately estimating microstructural measures. Future inquiries could further explore advanced spherical mean theory to refine the specificity and accuracy of microstructure imaging, thereby facilitating more detailed tissue characterization.

The SMT also inherently improves robustness to directional noise and gradient‐dependent artifacts, since averaging signals across diffusion directions reduces random fluctuations and motion‐induced inconsistencies. However, we acknowledge that severe motion or eddy‐current distortions may still affect the stability of the fitted SMS parameters (α, β, γ). Despite differences in preprocessing pipelines and acquisition conditions across datasets (e.g., HCP, BTC, SUDMEX), DEMIC maintains stable performance, as demonstrated in our cross‐dataset generalization experiments (Table 2; Figures 8 and 9). These results suggest that the learned microstructure codebook is resilient to moderate noise and preprocessing variability, supporting its robustness and generalization across heterogeneous acquisition protocols.

In this work, we selected the Mamba architecture to model long‐range spatial dependencies because it provides linear‐complexity selective state‐space modeling, which is particularly advantageous for capturing spatial‐sequential relationships in dMRI data. Unlike Transformers, which require quadratic attention computation, or LSTMs, which struggle with long‐range information propagation, Mamba employs dynamic state updates and continuous convolution operators that efficiently capture global contextual information while maintaining low memory usage. Recent studies (Zhu et al. ; Gu and Dao ; Ma, Li, and Wang ) have shown that Mamba‐based architectures can achieve results comparable to or better than those of Transformer‐based models across various visual tasks while offering significant computational benefits. Additionally, the effectiveness of Mamba in handling complex sequential data and facilitating multi‐granularity feature interactions has been validated in time‐series classification tasks (Zhang et al. ), providing theoretical support for the spatial‐sequential modeling employed in our framework. By combining CNNs for local feature extraction with Mamba for global spatio‐temporal modeling, DEMIC improves structural coherence and parameter stability during microstructure estimation.

Quantitative insights into the relative contributions of these architectural components are further evidenced by our ablation studies (Table S7). Specifically, the CNN component establishes the foundational representation of tissue microstructure by extracting local spatial features, such as fine‐grained anatomical boundaries and regional intensity variations. Building upon this foundation, the Mamba component provides substantial additional performance gains by modeling long‐range spatial‐sequential dependencies, thereby improving global structural consistency and stabilizing parameter estimation. To dynamically balance these local and global contributions, the spatial aggregation (SA) module serves as an adaptive integration mechanism. Rather than relying on fixed integration weights, it employs a learnable multi‐order gating strategy that assigns adaptive importance to features from different receptive fields. This adaptive weighting mechanism enables the network to automatically optimize the trade‐off between fine structural detail preservation and global coherence, significantly enhancing the robustness of parameter estimation under varying acquisition protocols and undersampling conditions.

Consistent with this enhanced robustness, our experiments demonstrate stable generalization across different acquisition protocols, including variations in gradient directions, sampling strategies, undersampling ratios, and b‐value distributions. Notably, the model trained on the SUDMEX dataset (b = 0, 1000, 3000 s/mm2) maintained high estimation accuracy when evaluated on the BTC dataset (b = 0, 700, 1200, 2800 s/mm2), despite discrepancies in the specific b‐values. These findings suggest that the proposed SMS‐based representation enables robust performance when training and testing b‐values lie within a comparable practical range. However, in more extreme out‐of‐distribution scenarios involving largely non‐overlapping b‐value ranges, some performance degradation may occur due to extrapolation beyond the observed attenuation regime. A systematic investigation of such extreme cross‐range generalization conditions remains an important direction for future work.

A key component of our framework is a novel parameterization method that focuses on the continuous representation of multi‐shell SMS data to improve generalizability across varying b‐value settings. In dMRI, several acquisition parameters—particularly b‐values and gradient directions—are critical for accurate microstructure estimation. A significant concern is the limitation of considering only b‐values while neglecting other dMRI parameters, such as echo time (TE) and diffusion time, which can also influence microstructural estimation, especially in complex tissue environments. However, our focus on b‐values was driven by their predominant role in established clinical protocols. Furthermore, the generalizability of our approach across different protocols is substantiated by our results, as evidenced by the BTC and SUDMEX datasets, which were acquired using protocols that differed significantly (e.g., b‐values, TE, matrix size, and voxel size). Notably, the generalization performance on the BTC dataset remains accurate (see Table 2; Figures 8 and 9). Future research endeavors could extend our parameterization method to incorporate higher‐dimensional joint modeling of b‐values, TE, diffusion time, and other acquisition parameters, potentially yielding a more comprehensive representation of dMRI.

Recent advancements in foundation models have opened exciting opportunities for brain image analysis. These emerging techniques have demonstrated the ability to learn transferable, generalizable representations from large‐scale datasets, enabling them to adapt to various downstream tasks with minimal fine‐tuning (Lei et al. ; Sadikov et al. ; Karimi ; Ma, He, et al. ). For instance, Karimi et al. developed a transformer‐based architecture trained using masked patch‐prediction and patch‐wise generation objectives. This model is a robust foundational encoder for diverse medical tasks, including segmentation, denoising, and structural quantification. Building upon the segment anything model (SAM) (Kirillov et al. ), MedSAM (Lei et al. ) has achieved remarkable success in medical image segmentation, accurately delineating anatomical structures and pathological regions with minimal fine‐tuning. Furthermore, Sadikov introduced a generative AI method based on Swin UNet Transformers, demonstrating the feasibility of denoising or super‐resolving dMRI scans across various acquisition schemes and populations, even with limited supervision, such as fine‐tuning on a single subject. These developments highlight a promising approach to estimating microstructures in dMRI, as foundation models effectively capture generalizable representations that can adapt to different microstructural indices and imaging conditions. Consistent with this perspective, our experimental results indicate that our learnable codebook framework is well‐positioned to serve as a foundation model for dMRI microstructure imaging. By pretraining on multiple large‐scale datasets, our codebook can capture generalizable tissue‐compartment kernels, facilitating both interpretable and adaptable microstructure estimation. This allows for efficient fine‐tuning to predict various microstructural indices with minimal data. Such adaptability makes our framework a promising step toward a unified foundation model for microstructural estimation, potentially benefiting a wide range of clinical and research applications.

In addition to leveraging large‐scale dMRI datasets, incorporating multiple modalities of information that have already been deconstructed can further enhance microstructural estimation. During dMRI scanning, other high‐resolution modalities are often acquired, such as T1‐weighted images (T1w). The information in the T1w modality better enables the network to exploit the relevance among input voxels (Qin, Li, et al. ). Through an attention module, Qin, Li, et al.  proposed a method for estimating HR tissue microstructure from LR dMRI data by incorporating the HR information from the T1w modality. Tissue microstructure can be better probed by integrating dMRI with complementary information from MR or non‐MR modalities. Liu and Özarslan  introduced several modalities, such as magnetic susceptibility, structural tensors, and internal field gradients, that can be integrated into dMRI techniques to improve the characterization of the underlying tissue microarchitecture and to provide insight into how dMRI signals relate to physiology and biology. Incorporating these complementary modalities may enable more accurate and biologically interpretable assessments of tissue microarchitecture. Our framework may be extended in future work to integrate such modalities, enabling more comprehensive, physiology‐informed microstructure imaging.

Despite its strong performance and flexibility, our method has several limitations that merit further exploration. First, although the proposed approach generalizes effectively across different protocols, its performance may decline when applied to datasets with markedly different scanner configurations, noise levels, or extreme undersampling conditions. This reveals a reliance on the distribution of the pretraining data and underscores the necessity for more diverse training cohorts to enhance robustness across varying datasets. Second, the current framework still necessitates fine‐tuning when adapting to entirely new microstructural models, suggesting that while the codebook's transferability is effective, it is not yet universally applicable. Third, most of our evaluations were conducted on healthy individuals and tumor cases; thus, broader validation involving neurological disorders such as multiple sclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, and traumatic brain injury is crucial to establish clinical generalizability and robustness in more heterogeneous tissue conditions.

The potential of our framework as a foundational model for microstructure imaging opens avenues for future research. Several directions could enhance the scalability and clinical value of our framework. By refining the codebook design and incorporating biophysical priors, we can improve the characterization of tissue microarchitecture and enhance interpretability, thereby supporting the development of new biophysical models. Additionally, integrating uncertainty quantification into the estimation process would help assess confidence in predictions, a critical aspect in clinical scenarios. Moreover, by leveraging the microstructure codebook learned by our model, we anticipate the development of diagnosis‐driven microstructure imaging—specifically, generating novel, disease‐specific microstructural indicators closely associated with specific conditions, thereby improving diagnostic specificity.
Materials and Methods
Spherical Mean‐Based Multi‐Compartment Microstructure Models

The SMT (Kaden, Kelm, et al. ; Kaden, Kruggel, and Alexander ) provides a robust framework for disentangling orientation‐dependent confounding factors in dMRI while preserving sensitivity to tissue microstructure (Neto Henriques ; Huynh et al. ; Wang et al. ; Wu ; Tristán‐Vega et al. ). MCMDI (Kaden, Kelm, et al. ) further extended SMT to accommodate multiple compartments. At its core, SMT leverages the spherical averaging of diffusion‐weighted signals across gradient directions to eliminate dependencies on fiber orientation dispersion. Building on this principle, DEMIC replaces the fixed, user‐defined spin model with a learnable microstructure codebook comprising 128 overcomplete tissue‐compartment kernels. These kernels mirror the spectrum‐of‐diffusion‐scales philosophy in SMSI (Huynh et al. ) and provide a data‐driven basis that maps the parameterized SMS (α, β, γ) to a diverse set of microstructural indices. This design not only enhances flexibility and generalizability across acquisition protocols but also preserves interpretability, as each kernel can be interpreted as encoding a specific diffusion‐scale pattern that contributes to the final microstructure estimates.
Parameterized Multi‐Shell Spherical Mean Signal

Previous study (Sun et al. ) has shown that the spherical‐mean signal (SMS) of different tissues exhibits heterogeneous exponential‐like decay patterns as b‐values increase, and such decay behavior provides discriminative tissue contrast. Inspired by the observation, we adopt a three‐parameter exponential formulation for SMS parameterization. This design provides a flexible yet compact encoding of SMS decay, striking a practical balance between expressiveness and stability; lower‐order models fail to capture heterogeneous attenuation across tissue types. At the same time, higher‐order formulations introduce unnecessary instability and noise sensitivity. Therefore, to learn a nonlinear mapping between SMS with arbitrary acquisition protocol and generalized microstructural maps, we parameterized the SMS  over the b‐values for each voxel using an e xponential model, as below:where  and  are the voxel‐wise coefficients estimated using a constrained non‐linear optimization algorithm. This parameterization makes the multi‐shell SMS representation independent of specific b‐values, thereby enhancing its generalizability.

In this study, SMS parameterization was performed using DWI data with b ≤ 3000 s/mm2, ensuring robust parameter estimation under practical acquisition conditions. Notably, the proposed formulation can naturally incorporate ultra‐high‐b‐value SMS if available. While ultra‐high‐b‐value data typically suffer from lower signal‐to‐noise ratios, the spherical mean technique effectively mitigates noise‐induced instability by averaging out directional variability, thereby preserving fitting stability. Including such measurements would further constrain the exponential fitting and enable more precise characterization of physical attenuation behavior in the high‐b regime, resulting in a more complete and accurate encoding of signal decay across the full b‐value spectrum. Furthermore, even when only low‐b SMS are used, the resulting parameterized representation implicitly captures the global attenuation trend, supporting accurate estimation of downstream microstructural indices (including those conventionally requiring ultra‐high‐b‐value acquisitions).

Several methodological improvements were incorporated to ensure the stability and robustness of the fitting process. First, a preprocessing step was performed to eliminate invalid voxels. The DWIs were initially masked to exclude non‐brain regions, ensuring that only meaningful tissue voxels were processed. Additionally, voxels with negative or near‐zero signal intensities were identified and excluded to prevent numerical instability during fitting.

We employed constrained nonlinear least‐squares optimization with the Python library for the fitting. Since the optimization algorithm may require many iterations to converge without proper initialization, leading to instability in whole‐brain fitting, we introduced an initialization strategy to improve convergence efficiency. Specifically, we set  = 0, reducing the model to . Given the first b‐value  and its corresponding signal , along with another b‐value  and its signal , we derived initial estimates  and  to serve as starting points for the optimization process. This initialization significantly reduced the required number of function evaluations and improved the convergence stability of voxel‐wise fitting. Additionally, the fitting procedure was performed under constraints to ensure stable, robust parameter estimation. Specifically, α was constrained to be positive to maintain physically valid signal intensities, β was restricted to be between 0 and 0.007 based on prior observations from healthy subjects, and  was enforced to be non‐negative to prevent negative signal offsets. Failed fits were assigned default values of 0 and flagged as outliers. Since the fitting process was conducted independently for each voxel, we implemented parallel computing using the Python joblib library to accelerate whole‐brain fitting by leveraging multiple threads (e.g., 32), thereby significantly reducing computational time.

Following fitting, the resulting parameter maps  can be normalized to the unbiased parameterized map  with a mean of 0 and variance of 1 for each of the coefficient maps separately using the z‐score, which reduces the variation of signal amplitude across subjects. Finally, the processed parameter maps were stored in NIfTI format for subsequent analysis.

As shown in Figure 1, in DEMIC, we first compute the multi‐shell SMS by averaging the diffusion‐weighted signals across gradient directions within each b‐shell. To obtain a compact, protocol‐agnostic representation, the voxel‐wise multi‐shell SMS across b‐values is fitted using our three‐parameter exponential model, yielding α, β, γ. These parameters are concatenated slice‐wise and serve as the direct input to the hybrid Mamba‐CNN network.
Hybrid Mamba‐CNN Architecture for Joint Microstructure Learning

We propose a hybrid Mamba‐CNN network architecture for high‐quality estimation of microstructural metrics. Specifically, we introduce the Vim‐CNN block, which integrates convolutional operations with the Vision Mamba (Zhu et al. ) (Vim Block) module to extract multi‐scale structural information, enabling more precise microstructural analysis. The Vim‐CNN block consists of three branches that process the input feature map (C,H,W): (1) Vim Block branch: This branch passes the input through the Vim Block without changing the spatial resolution or number of channels. The Vim block leverages Mamba's sequence modeling capabilities to capture long‐range spatial dependencies across the image, providing complementary global context to convolutional processing. The output of this branch retains the original shape (C,H,W). (2) Convolution branch: This branch consists of two consecutive 3 × 3 convolutional layers, each with padding = 1, preserving the spatial dimensions. These layers emphasize local structural patterns and short‐range feature interactions. The channel size remains unchanged throughout the branch, outputting features of shape (C,H,W). (3) Residual branch: A 1 × 1 convolutional layer is applied to the input, projecting the feature channels from C to 2C, while keeping the spatial dimensions unchanged. This branch is a residual transformation path that provides a shortcut connection to stabilize training and enhance feature fusion flexibility. The outputs from the Vim Block and 3 × 3 Convolution branches are concatenated along the channel dimension and summed with the Residual branch output, forming a fused feature of size (2C,H,W). This hybrid structure enables efficient integration of local and global representations.

Codebook Learning for Microstructure Estimation

To enable a robust and extensible framework for microstructural metric estimation, we propose a model that learns a microstructural codebook—a set of 128 overcomplete tissue‐compartment kernels that capture a spectrum of diffusion scales. Conceptually, the codebook parallels the principle of spherical mean spectrum imaging (SMSI), where the spherical‐mean signal is decomposed into contributions from multiple diffusion scales. In DEMIC, instead of using a hand‐crafted or user‐defined spin model, these kernels are learned directly from data, forming a data‐driven dictionary that maps the parameterized SMS representation (α, β, γ) to diverse microstructural indices. Practically, the codebook corresponds to the 128‐channel latent representation produced after hierarchical feature expansion and refinement within the Mamba‐CNN backbone. Each channel behaves as a learned tissue‐compartment kernel, encoding a distinct diffusion scale. The 128‐dimensional tensor is then fed into a 1 × 1 convolutional output layer to predict the desired microstructural indices. This makes the term “codebook” appropriate: it functions as a compact dictionary that encodes diffusion behaviors and supports an interpretable, shared representation across multiple biophysical models.

Inspired by recent studies (Huynh et al. ; Wu ), we decompose the input coefficient map into nearly independent tissue‐compartment kernels. Specifically, a 1 × 1 convolution layer first increases the channel dimension from 3 to 32, followed by four consecutive Vim‐CNN blocks that progressively double the channel width from 32 to 512. This hierarchical expansion enables the network to extract increasingly expressive representations for microstructural modeling by combining convolutional operations and the Vision Mamba, improving microstructural estimation accuracy. We further incorporate a sequence of Vim and SA blocks to enhance the representational capacity of these 512‐channel features. Following this refinement, a 1 × 1 convolutional layer is applied to compress the 512‐channel feature space to a 128‐channel representation, thereby reducing redundancy and yielding a compact, expressive microstructural codebook. This codebook can be interpreted as a set of learned compartment kernels that flexibly characterize a wide range of microstructural metrics across multiple dMRI models.

We further enhance the representation capability of the learned codebook by introducing an auxiliary reconstruction path that synthesizes the input  and  maps from the codebook via a 1 × 1 convolution layer, which introduces an additional reconstruction loss. This reconstruction loss (weighted by 0.1) guides the learning process and ensures that the dictionary retains information relevant to the original SMS coefficients. The final output head is implemented as an additional 1 × 1 convolutional layer for downstream microstructure estimation, integrating the microstructural codebook into the desired microstructure indices. This design enables joint characterization of multiple indices during training. It enables rapid adaptation to novel models via a simple modification to the final output layer and fine‐tuning, ensuring strong extensibility and transferability. By default, this layer outputs 24 channels corresponding to 24 microstructural indices derived from eight widely used biophysical diffusion models.
Microstructure Transfer Learning

The microstructural codebook established by our framework provides a shared and expressive representation that can be reused across microstructural models. As illustrated in Figure 2, DEMIC first learns a generalizable microstructure codebook during pre‐training on large‐scale datasets, where the network maps the parameterized SMS inputs (α, β, γ) to a diverse set of microstructural indices. When adapting to a previously unseen dMRI model, only the final 1 × 1 convolution head is replaced to match the number of target indices, and fine‐tuning is performed using a limited amount of labeled data. This workflow enables efficient transfer to unseen microstructural metrics by utilizing the shared representation encoded in the codebook, significantly reducing data requirements and enhancing the framework's applicability across diverse clinical and research domains.

In the pre‐training phase, the model was trained on 200 randomly selected subjects from the Human Connectome Project (HCP), with 10% reserved for validation. The parameterized SMS inputs (α, β, γ) were computed from all available DWIs, including 18, 90, 90, and 90 DWIs at b = 0, 1000, 2000, and 3000 s/mm2, respectively (288 DWIs in total). The reference microstructural targets consisted of 13 indices derived from five models—MSDKI, FwDTI, IVIM, WMTI, and 3D‐SHORE—estimated using complete DWI data with standard toolboxes to obtain high‐quality ground truth. Specifically, the estimated indices included: FW, F, uFA, MSK, DI, mean signal diffusion, hindered AD, hindered RD, AWF, axonal diffusivity, MSD, RTOP, and f. The network was trained for 100 epochs with a batch size of 8 and a learning rate of 5 × 10−4. During this phase, the network assimilated a comprehensive microstructural codebook composed of 128 overcomplete tissue‐compartment kernels. To adapt the model to an unfamiliar dMRI technique, such as SANDI, we replace the final 1 × 1 convolutional layer with a head that matches the number of target dMRI indices. For instance, when adapting to SANDI, which derives six metrics, the output head is modified to produce a 6‐channel output. Next, we fine‐tune the model using a limited dataset. This simple modification enables us to estimate previously unseen microstructure metrics accurately. Microstructure Architecture

We use eight common microstructure models in clinical research to learn an overcomplete set of tissue‐compartment kernels from the mapping between spherical mean signals and microstructural features. NODDI (Zhang et al. ) detects the microstructure of dendrites and axons and provides a sensible estimation of neurite density and orientation dispersion. FwDTI (Henriques et al. ) extends the DTI model by adding a compartment to remove free‐water effects and then infers tissue macroscopic geometry from water displacement. MSDKI (Neto Henriques ) characterizes kurtosis from average signals across all directions, which does not depend on fiber‐distribution functions, thereby overcoming the limitations of conventional DKI. IVIM (Wong et al. ) quantifies the diffusion properties of water molecules in static tissue and blood flow, enabling simultaneous assessment of microvascular and parenchymal abnormalities in the brain. WMTI (Fieremans et al. ) estimates the contributions of hindered and restricted diffusion in well‐aligned fibers and allows estimation of the axonal volume fraction and the diffusion extracellular tortuosity, thereby distinguishing axonal loss from myelin degeneration processes. 3D‐SHORE (Merlet and Deriche ) is an extension of the 1D‐SHORE basis, which models the signal as a summation of continuous functions, thereby enabling the characterization of the analytical average diffusivity within a voxel, the probability that water molecules undergoing diffusion will return to their original position after a specified time interval, and so on. Note that our method supports other microstructure frameworks in a flexible manner. DKI+ (Zhang et al. ) provides a more sophisticated characterization of restricted water diffusion, enabling the exploration of non‐Gaussian diffusion metrics in tissues with complex components and structures. MCMDI (Kaden, Kelm, et al. ) models non‐Gaussian diffusion in brain tissue and employs the spherical mean technique to remove the effects of orientation dispersion, yielding estimates of the neurite density index of the intra‐ and extra‐neurite compartments in the brain.
Dataset

Our experiments used five open datasets (SUDMEX (Angeles‐Valdez et al. ), BTC Preop/Postop (Aerts et al. ), HCP (Van Essen et al. ), and HCP‐MGH (Fan et al. )) across different acquisition protocols and health conditions. Table 4 shows the detailed acquisition parameters.

passages.text: The SUDMEX dataset includes 60 healthy controls (HC) and 69 subjects with cocaine use disorder. BTC preop/postop includes 10 HCs and 25 preoperative brain tumor patients, as well as 10 HCs and 19 postoperative patients. The HCP dataset contains 1200 subjects, and the HCP‐MGH dataset includes 35 subjects. For all datasets, the training–validation split was performed randomly with an 80/20 ratio. For each subject, the reference microstructural maps (ground truth) used for training and quantitative evaluation were obtained by voxel‐wise fitting of the corresponding biophysical models to high‐quality/full‐sampling data. Specifically, we used different toolboxes: FwDTI, MSDKI, WMTI, and 3D‐SHORE models were fitted using DIPY (Garyfallidis et al. ); NODDI and SANDI metrics were estimated via AMICO (Daducci et al. ); IVIM and MCMDI metrics were obtained using DMIPY (Fick et al. ); and DKI+ metrics were derived using MK‐Curve (Zhang et al. ). For the SANDI model that requires high‐b data, the reference maps were generated using the available high‐b shells (5000 and 10,000 s/mm2) from the HCP‐MGH dataset.

All datasets were processed using well‐established pipelines. For both SUDMEX and BTC, we used a combined FSL (v5.0.9) and MRtrix3 pipeline including MP‐PCA denoising, Gibbs ringing removal, motion/eddy correction, susceptibility distortion correction, and bias‐field correction. HCP and HCP‐MGH data were preprocessed using their official pipelines, including advanced motion, distortion, and gradient nonlinearity corrections. No spatial normalization to a group template was performed; all analyses were carried out in native diffusion space. Importantly, no voxel resizing or interpolation was applied. Differences in voxel size were handled implicitly via padded 3 × 3 convolutions (padding = 1), which preserve spatial dimensions and allow the network to process all datasets without resampling.
Implementation

The proposed network was implemented in PyTorch v2.1.0 and trained on two NVIDIA GeForce 4090 GPUs for 200 epochs using the ADAM optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001 and a batch size of 8. The optimization minimized the mean absolute error (MAE) for microstructure estimation and parameterized SMS reconstruction, with the latter weighted by 0.1. During fine‐tuning, only the microstructure estimation loss was optimized.

To benchmark our network, we compared it with several existing methods: MLP (Golkov et al. ), CNN (HashemizadehKolowri et al. ), UNET (Falk et al. ), UNET++ (Zhou et al. ), and MESC‐SD (Ye et al. ), all of which utilized the native dMRI signal. While JEAMP (Wang et al. ), and our method take three parameterized SMS coefficient maps as input. For fairness, each baseline was constrained to the set of microstructural indices defined in its original implementation: MLP estimates six indices (FWF, NDI, ODI, AK, MK, RK); CNN estimates 11 (FA, MD, RD, AK, MK, RK, ODI, FWF, NDI, Vint, λ); UNET, UNET++, and MESC‐SD estimate three (FWF, NDI, ODI); and JEAMP estimates 16 indices following its published configuration. These methods were initially designed for specific biophysical models and lack protocol‐agnostic generalization, cross‐model extensibility, or mechanisms for capturing long‐range spatial dependencies, which limits their scalability beyond their native index sets. The presence of NaN values in our tables and figures reflects cases in which these baselines were unable to estimate specific indices due to inherent design constraints. Quantitative assessments between the prediction and reference were implemented using PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS (Zhang et al. ), and NRMSE. In addition to PSNR and SSIM, we incorporated Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS)—a deep‐feature–based perceptual metric that evaluates semantic and structural fidelity by comparing activations extracted from a pre‐trained neural network. Unlike traditional pixel‐level similarity metrics, LPIPS captures perceptual realism and has been increasingly validated for medical imaging applications. Recent research (Breger et al. ) has further demonstrated its superiority over PSNR and SSIM in assessing perceptual and clinical relevance. To complement LPIPS, we also report normalized root‐mean‐square error (NRMSE) to quantify voxel‐wise deviation in a scale‐independent form. Together, these four metrics provide a more comprehensive evaluation of microstructure estimation accuracy and perceptual consistency. The brain mask was applied to restrict comparisons to the brain region for all metrics, ensuring fair evaluation and avoiding background influence.
